Happy Friday.
Happy Friday.
I seem to, so far, the trend is that we only talk on Fridays.
Oh, nice.
I don't know.
Is it nice?
I don't know.
I think you talk.
Great.
was really, it was really great to talk.
You're growing the company and how you're thinking about kind of all the people that you're hiring.
so that gave me some good, some good context.
Nice.
How are you thinking overall?
I'm sure you're talking to other companies as well.
And just kind of thinking about like next steps for you overall.
How are you feeling and thinking about things?
Yeah, I would say that in general, where my head's at is, so I am talking to several different companies.
And I think where my head is at is like the biggest questions for me.
Coming into this meeting are really, are twofold.
The first is, how are you thinking about growthx and positioning it less as a service and like as an agency and more as a product?
And like, what does that path look like?

And then the second piece is like, on the team, and I mean, this is some of the stuff that I asked, that I talked about with Bridget as well, but like, on the team side, like, how like, what are the things that you're trying to test in terms of team structure?

And how are you thinking about it from because so she talked about the like, content manager, the editor, the managing director, but I'm thinking about like, as you grow the company, like, how are people going to specialize?
What do the different pods look like?
Like, what is kind of going through your mind?
As it stands to it.
So those are like the two biggest things.
Cool.
That's super helpful.
Well, we can, we can start there as well.
I think
And then we'd love to kind of jump in and dig in a little bit as well on your side.
So I think it's a little bit of a counter position that we have, which is that services is an advantage, not a disadvantage.
And I think we're going to be quite misunderstood for a little while.

But as our board member and investor and lead investor said, what gets me to be excited about your business today is what I'm worried about if it's what your business looks like several years from now, but also vice versa.
I would be more worried right now if you're trying to be softer today and you're trying to be just standard SaaS model today because it's missing kind of the whole premise.
So I think starting with the counter position, and I guess it's a question for me.
Like, why do you think your last company didn't do well from a product market fit?
Or I think there must have been something that didn't go according to plan in order for it to not be growing more or hiring people versus laying off people, right?
So I'm curious, and I'll connect the dots a little bit, but I'm curious just from your perspective, from like distribution growth and product market fit, I guess.
Yeah, I think a couple things come to mind.
think the first is, we had, there was clearly an incumbent.
And we were going against an incumbent.
And I think we, even before my time, during my time, there was a, like, very much.
It was called Airflow.
So Airflow is the open source tool.
And so there's Astronomer, which is a company that basically, like, hosts Airflow.
for you and does a bunch of stuff around it.
then also like Astronomer's biggest competitors are Airflow.
There's like a Google Cloud Composer, which is like within Google Cloud, like their own service that's basically just managing that tool.
And so it's Airflow to all of its like cloud versions.
And I think within the company, there's definitely a very much a moving target of like, how do we compare against them?
Like, how are we 10x better?
Like, how do we position that in the market?
How do we talk about it?
And so it went from we don't talk about Airflow, because they're not worth talking about to we need to talk about Airflow all the time.
And if we don't, that's a mistake, right?
And so I think there was just like, a lot of moving targets.

But I think that from from that perspective, I think the reason that it hindered us was like, if there is an incumbent, you have to be you have to make it very clear why people should choose you over the incumbent or switch over to you from the incumbent.
And that switching cost is oftentimes very high.
And we never really like,
Consider that as a company, and I think that was really a mistake.
So that was, I think, the first thing.
I think the second thing was that we had a lot of, we had technical founders that were very developer-focused on, like, the open source model or whatever, right?
But it's like, are you building an open source product or are you building a business?
And at some point, you have to choose one or the other.
Like, one has to take priority, and I think we were trying to do both, and we weren't ever clear on which one we were prioritizing, anything that really inhibited us.
So, for example, like, the product is very free.
The free tier is very free.
Like, there's, you know, we say, like, oh, it's not for production workloads, but, like, there are plenty of production workloads that you can run on the free tier and in open source.
And I think that we're
That a mistake.
I think that no matter how good the product is, like, if you give away too many things for free, you're just not going to make as much money as you could otherwise.
And so I think that, like, that was a big oversight.
I think that, like, just this year, right, like, we started working on that.
started working on both of those things.
I think it was just a little bit too late.
And I think that's why there was a layoff.
And I still think that those are the two biggest factors in making pretty much successful.
Yeah.
And that's super helpful.
So to kind of flip that to back to your question, Ray, like how to think about service versus product.
Starting with service is, like, the biggest advantage because a few things.
One, someone is paying you for something that they value.
So you know exactly how much they value that because it's a service and they want the job to be done.
And second, you know exactly what needs to be done in order to deliver.
Because you need to deliver the service, right?
And you don't care if it's through a UI, you don't care if it's through automation, through AI, through humans, or a combination of any of those things, you need to deliver this, right?
So what ends up happening in our case is like when we're saying, hey, we're to be your entire, for now, content and growth engine as a service team, right?
And we're delivering high quality content at scale with the right strategy to help you grow, for example.
Then the people delivering that are actually our customers.

And we're developing, you want to call it product, want to call whatever you want to call it, enablement plus product, plus automation, plus anything else, plus tooling, to enable them to deliver that highly efficiently, right?

And, but the cool thing with this, and that was just, we just had a leadership upside is like, that means that every single minute of engineering time is spent on things that already have product market fit.
And, and the.
And there's literally zero sunken costs.

There's no, there's never a situation right now where we're building something that might never be used or might never deliver any value to our customers because our customers are the people delivering the value to our customers, right?
Like, in other words, like, you know, everyone has said this company is the customer.
And the other part, too, is like it enables us to extract way more value.
So if we took exactly what we're doing and we somehow build this amazing platform, then all those things really, like, you know, perfectly.
One, you need to explain to people how to use it.
You need to teach them how to use it.
And even then, you would not be able to extract the same amount of value.
probably remove a zero from our ACV, right?
Which is great because then you're like, okay, well, but I can reach this crazy distribution.
But if that was true, like, there's very actually few products lately, like, you know, that reach, like, that level of, like, going crazy viral, right?
Like, so.
So for us, we're projecting to end this year at 76% to 79% gross margin, and then the following year, end at 91% gross margin.
Obviously, I don't want to be a 91% gross margin.
We should probably be investing more in different functions and kind of thinking through.
But regardless, the business is already software efficient.

And so the difference is we're able to extract a lot more and close deals a lot faster because it's service and learn a lot more, but also not make mistakes of like, hey, let me build this entire UI, assuming someone is going to want to use it a certain way and do a certain thing and extract value a certain way.
But with that, like, it's the way to think about it is the service is a product, the platform that our delivery teams use is a product, the infrastructure underneath the hood.
you.
is a product.
Our go-to-market is a product as well.

And so if you think about all those things as products, like there's a lot of different products to work on, which is kind of exciting, you know, but like in a lot of ways, like we are not just trying to innovate on the products themselves, but also the business model itself, which is like, to me, like the most energizing thing, which is like, okay, like you can be a service, but then you can also have a cursor-like platform, but then you can also monetize all the different APIs that we have, but you can also expose those APIs, or you can take some of our framework and open source it.

But also you can take this model and literally go acquire like a, you know, a recruiting agency and give them our platform that we're using to recruit internally and do a version of growthx for recruiting.
Or, right, like you can, there's so many different aspects of this.
And that's, I think the part that's like really exciting is like, if you can.
That's kind of compounding effect.
But also a couple of inspirations for me, like Tiny, I don't know if you're familiar with the company, it's a public company.
They own Dribble and a bunch of others.
There's a ton of podcasts and things about them.
They're highly profitable and they're like a portfolio of companies.

You have, obviously, Amazon and AWS, and then their ability to just build multiple businesses without disrupting their current businesses, but in a way that one reinforces the other in a lot of ways, right?
And in a way that everything they use internally becomes a product externally as well.
But even how they do that is that internally, everything has to be an API with good documentation for anyone to use it, right?
So it's not that different from that, you know?
And so in some ways, we should be very thoughtful about how and when to expose and when to take some risks.
And I mean, we could take any of our APIs and launch it as a SaaS, but I would probably want to do it as a separate brand.
To not cannibalize our own, right?
Or not even maybe disclose right away that it's, like, by growthx, right?
Like, and so, like, there's a lot of things we can do to experiment a ton, you know?
And I think that's part of the fun as well.
Yeah, that, no, that makes a lot of sense.
And I think, right, there are a few companies in the data space that I'm familiar with that, like, actually also started on a similar model.
Like, DBT comes to mind.
started a service.
DBT Labs is one of our inspirations as well.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
So they started a service.
And they're like, wait, we built this really great internal product.
Like, we should do something with it.
And, you know, didn't raise money for a really long time because they were profitable and then chose to because they wanted to, like, scale the product.
So that makes a lot of sense.
I guess my question is, like, a year is a really long time for startup or in startup world.
But, like, a year from now, like, are you, do you want to stay services?
Or do you want to, at some point, sell a product?
Like, is, like, at some
On which side does the aspiration fall?
All of the above.
It's just that we have to be really careful in the audiences too, right?
On the investor audience, there are certain things that you don't want to over-promise, but you also don't want to under-promise.
You don't want to paint a crazy picture that then people are like, whoa, you have no focus, but you also don't want to be like, we're just this and that's it, right?
And at the same time, internally, it's kind of the similar balance point of where you don't want to just tell the whole company is like, hey, we're working on 15 different products here soon.
And people are like, wait, wait, hold on.
And so I think that's the balancing act for me.
It's like I need to think as a CEO three to five years out.
Product engineer needs to be thinking 18 months out.
So, you know, marketing sales and delivery team should be probably thinking three to six months at most, if not like week of, month of as well.
So, Thank
Like in having that balance.
But we can't have everyone thinking like five years out or in AI terms, right?
Like it's like a year out too much, you know.
But I would say like the hope is things kind of slap you in the face and are yanked out of you.
So the strongest signal that the product market fit is when customers are trying to get it out of your hands.

So right now, Vista Equity Partners is like, please have access to your platform to build it for all the sales use cases, which you've already built a bunch of those for yourself and starting with one company and then our whole portfolio, right?
Like one of our customers, Galileo AI, is like, hey, you're already doing deep research from topics.
Can we use that for sales?
Whereas post-call or pre-call processing, right?
And it's just like, yes, we could.
Should we do that?
Should we do it as a service?
Should we do it as a platform?
Right?
So there's a lot of those that I think.
I think we're getting demand and we can expose, but doing so now can be detrimental because we're extracting so much value and we can grow and we're in this very clear path.
So for me, priority number one right now is making sure that we're delivering overwhelming value to our customers and we find a clear path to guide them throughout their journey of growth.
And as they go and graduate from one or two work streams to three or four, like that we're helping them all grow and we have amazing stories to tell.

And then in the process of doing that, continue to butcher our frameworks and like our infrastructure and everything we're doing and then launch some kind of experience this year before we do a series B.
And so for me, I think like most likely it will be something that will feel like Bolt.new or lovable a little bit, but more useful like cursor is useful, right?
Like I think the difference.
It's like, like, lovable and others are like, cool, and very viral, but not as useful.
Whereas like cursor is like, highly, highly, highly, highly useful, but not flashy.
And you're just like, well, this is just like, you know, totally.
And then after you use it for like a month, and use it well, you're like, , don't take this away from me ever.
Like, you know, yeah, 100% want that for knowledge work, you know, and I love it.

think it was 2021 or something where you're like, Terraform for data.

Except one of the things Terraform didn't do well.

Like, so one of the insights at HashiCorp when I was there was like, the whole idea was like, eventually, and I remember Kevin Fishner, he's a friend, and you know, he was VP of product, VP of marketing, sales.
He did every function in the company.
And like, the whole idea was like, if you have all these

Like Terraform and you have also all the data on your networking and like all the compute, you know, you have all the insights and you should be able to then, you know, auto manage and learn from all your infrastructure, right?
That never happened.
Like they never brought all those things together.
And so it was always too like separate.
But at one point that was the vision for it, right?
Like they had such like a forward thinking like Mitchell, you know, and actually we just, hired Clint, who was engineer number six and built most of Terraform and a lot of Terraform providers.

yeah.

think there's like, you know, there's a trend in venture capital where it's like, build and they will come.

defensible thing today?
Like, if you're a founder starting a company, right?
Like, what do you think is the most defensible thing you can do early on?
That's a good question.
Honestly, I think that so one of the things that I was reading about is like, I think, like with a lot of AI tools, there's like, there's a lot of churn.
Like, is it a bubble?
Is it not?
Right?
Where you're promised this, like, you know, beautiful, like, AISDR or this, this right sort of thing.
And then you try it out and you're like, oh, the quality does not meet, does not meet the standard.
I
And then there's churn.

I think, honestly, like, the ability to go from, to have someone have a click of what you can do with the product, and then the quality, like, even if it's for a very small sliver, I think that a lot of, one of, right, one of the things that I was talking about at Prefect was like, there's a lot of, like, I don't know, like, leadership ADHD, we wanted to do everything all at once, but that means we didn't really, like, do any one thing really well.

And so I think focus and, like, going from zero to 100 in, like, one very specific sliver, it just has to be the way, because then you prove out that value as opposed to, like, failing because you didn't try hard enough, which I think is, like, the worst way to fail, because then you don't actually know if, you would have put all your, like, eggs in that basket, like, if you would have actually succeeded.
Yeah, exactly.
Like, in some ways, like, what is a job to be done?
with my Casey with Readiness, time
That you can be vertically integrated in order to create that, like, amazing experience and then expand from there, right?

Like, you're not going to create the iPhone-like experience by, like, you know, taking on a bunch of different things, but you still need to be, like, vertically integrated and then building additional things that kind of add to the ecosystem, you know?
Yeah, that makes sense.

And just, like, switching gears just a little bit, one of the things that I always like doing is kind of, like, digging super deep into, like, background, and I'm just pulling up your profile real quick.
It's a fun one that I actually learned when I was interviewing at Grammarly a while back.
ended up going to work for HashiCorp instead, but they did this kind of interview style, which was just, like, asking questions about someone.
Like, kind of journey, you know?
And it was, like, a lot of fun.
But I think, like, before your previous role, maybe we can start at PerPay.
Yeah, so tell me more about that role.
Like, why did you join the company?
And yeah, just give me a little bit more context on the role initially.
Yeah, totally.
So the role at PerPay was I was their first data engineering hire.
And so they had, so PerPay is a buy now, pay later tool, but also an e-commerce platform.
And so there was, like, if you can imagine on the buy now, pay later side, like, underwriting, a lot of data, like, you have to make sure to manage risk.
And so they had a data scientist to kind of help power the underwriting algorithm.
But then it was about, like, like, that was kind of the first place where scaling it and making sure there's the proper data infrastructure to support it.
Because if it went down, there were just,
And very drastic company implications if it wasn't right because of the data error.
And so I joined the company because I liked being that first data engineering hire.
It was the position I was in at the company before that.
And I think it was more of just like that because it allowed me to architect as opposed to like just take on someone else's architecture.
I really liked that like bigness of data engineering on the architecture side.
I also really like I connected with the I connected with who was my manager at the time, who was the who's now the who's promoted to the I think the chief strategy officer.
He was like the VP of analytics or something like that.
And he just had like a very data driven approach.
He was just like very methodical about how he approached the company and the strategy of the company and so really appreciated that just that approach.
And, yeah, so I think it was really interesting.
There was a lot of data to work with.
Like, whenever you're in B2C, there's just, like, running A-B tests is so much faster because you have so many more data points.
And so that was pretty fun.
Yeah, that's really cool.
And tell me more about, like, kind of, like, the first 90 days.
What was your mandate?
You know, like, you know, you kind of joined.
sounded like your manager.
It was very methodical, which is awesome.
You know, and you have a ton of data.
I'm sure that means also, like, a ton of problems or a ton of things to build or architect, you know.
So I'm curious on, like, when you first joined, what was your mandate and how did you approach it?
Yeah, so I'm trying to remember.
So the first 90 days, so the big first project that I took on was automating their invoicing flow.
So how they did, like, accounts payable and refunds.
That was a big.
Topic, right?
Like on the e-commerce side, and then how they paid their vendors, stuff like that.
On the accounting side, that was very manual.
was basically all through like Google Sheets, and it was just like a lot of copy and pasting.
was very inefficient.
And so my mandate was like to make it better.
And so it started out very vague, where it was like, okay, like walk through what exists today.
Like what is the input and what does the output need to be?
And then it was really just like my creativity around like, okay, how do we actually get from input to output?
And so that was really interesting.
But I think throughout that, what ended up happening was like, I tacked on work for myself, because throughout that, I like, they had, right, an orchestra, they had a data architecture of some sort.
And I was like, oh my god, like, we need some code linting.
Like we need some basic stuff to like make this like, able to use for like some repeatability.
And so I did a bunch of like, more like engineering.
Like CICD work, I guess, throughout that, like, as a byproduct of that, like, actual project.
So that was pretty fun.
So that's cool.

And so in your time there, like, what do you think was, like, your proudest accomplishment or project or thing that was, like, looking back, like, you think of it and you're like, oh, that was really cool or that you feel good about, you know?
Yeah.
So my biggest project by far was I replatformed our data warehouse.
And but so we were using Redshift, which was very slow.
And we're having a lot of bottlenecks and a lot of issues and reporting because of that.
But we wanted to I actually don't know that.
So we wanted to go a data lake approach because we wanted to cross use the data for analytics and for like the underwriting system, which is basically hooking up into like.
The actual product and needed to be used real-time, so it couldn't necessarily be in a warehouse.
so in hindsight, we ended up choosing an S3-based data lake, basically.
In hindsight, we probably could have also gone Snowflake, but there was a whole procurement issue, so who knows if that would have actually happened.
But the reason that I was proud of that is because really two reasons.
The first is it was through that project where I actually got introduced to Terraform and on the infrastructure side and how to – it's not just about where the data store.
It's like, okay, how do you manage all the infrastructure and how do you hook everything together and how do you make sure that everything is efficient and works?
And so I got introduced to a lot of concepts and then basically made the data team way, way closer to how the software engineering team worked at the company.
And so that, like, relationship between the data team…
And the actual engineering team, I think, became a lot stronger through that.
The second reason is that in actually re-platforming from Redshift to the S3-backed data lake, first of all, we did that with no downtime, which I think was really awesome.
And that was an engineering effort in itself.
And I think the second thing is being able to do that so efficiently that we didn't duplicate data, but we stored it in one place to be used for analytics.

And to be pulled in for, so to be used in analytics, to be pulled in model training for the underwriting model, and then also to be used in, like, the product recommendation system that was in the product.
And so I think the amount of efficiency, like, there was a lot of forethought, but I think that paid off in the efficiency and the outcome.
That's cool.

Zach.
Zach.

Zach.

So that type of relationship worked really well, and I really liked that.
I think that he also went to that for me as an employee.

I would say that in terms of what I think he could do differently, I think that he, honestly, there's like, not too much.
He was a really, really, really great manager.
And I really enjoyed working with him.

So, so then, um, I think around like March, 2022 is when you left, like, um, like what was the trigger like that you think got you to, to want to leave or do something else?
Yeah.

So I think the first was, um, Herpe was, um, a fully in-person company in Philly.
And, um, I wanted to move out of Philly by the end of the year.
And so I knew that.
My time at PerPay was finite because they just like, that was just the situation.
And I made that pretty known to them.
And so then, right, I went down this path of like, okay, well, if not PerPay, what do I want to do?
And I realized, like, I kind of maintained throughout my time there, like, I don't really want to be a data engineer anywhere else.
I think that I have found the place where I am happiest as a data engineer.
Like, I think that the culture for data was really, really strong there.
And I really liked that.
And I also kind of grew to a point in my data career where, like, I was a savvy enough engineer that, like, I didn't, I was by no means an expert in everything.
For sure not.
But I, like, got to a point where I knew the right questions to ask to figure something out.
And at that point, I was like, okay, well, I'm not, I can't directly time my work to business.
business value.
And I feel like I, you know, at some point, within the next 30 years, if I continue down this path, like, I'm going to get to be an expert in a lot of these things.
And like, I don't want to find that cap.
And so how do I ensure that I put myself in a position where there are continuously new problems to solve and continuously new things to learn, because learning is really important to me.
And so I realized that I no longer wanted to be a data engineer.
And I was like, okay, what next?
So there's a couple things I knew that I was like, oh, like marketing is interesting, but I didn't want to jump into anything specific.
I was at, so during COVID, I was like bored.
And I started like writing technical blogs and stuff.
so then people started reaching out to me like, hey, like, you should write for us.
And so I was contracting was making like decent money, just like writing for all sorts of different companies, mostly in the data space, because what I knew.
But then I was like, oh, like, I can do this more.
And so then I was like, okay, well, I'm gonna, I'm gonna,

Um, and so I started doing that.
So I left and I didn't have a full-time role lined up.
Um, and I was like, I'm going to freelance.
I also was like, you know what, if I were to start a company, maybe now's the time.
And so I, um, was, uh, exploring, I didn't name the company.
I had like, uh, um, an engineer that I was working with and we were like, okay, let's see if we can start a company together.
And we had this, um, idea for basically like a more engineering first approach to marketing, to marketing operations.
Um, and so that came from like my time, um, doing data engineering for the marketing team.
And, um, I would say that like, in hindsight, there were a lot of flaws to the approach.
I think we were both on like, here's what should exist, but it's like, who's actually going to use it?
What is the urgency?
It's like on, from a sales perspective, there were a lot of questions.
And so I understand why that didn't really go.
Very far, but I learned a lot in the process.
So I'm very thankful that I did that.
But then I was like, wow, I have so much to learn in marketing and sales.
And like, this is something that I can contribute a lot to from my technical background.
And so that's kind of what landed me at Prefect.
So that was like that kind of eight month journey between March and then December when I started Prefect.
That's really cool.
I love that.
I have so many thoughts in here, but I'll stick to this.
Are you okay if we go over a little?
just want to, I know, but I moved my other meetings too.
Okay.
So, so then like, why it sounded like you were writing freelancing, potentially start a new company.
And then around December of that year, right?
Like you join your more recent experience.
Free fact.
then it's like, what, what, what triggered that?
Or how did that happen?
Yeah, I would say that I was, so there are two things I would say from my own professional journey, I think I just.
I realized that like, yeah, I could figure out marketing and sales on my own, but I could also work at a company and learn from incredible people.
And I think for me, was like, this is a time when like, I want to learn from other people.
And I kind of noticed that in myself.
And I was like, I could really benefit from this.

And I really missed, I think what I missed about full time work that you don't really get in consulting, especially when you're like truly freelancing alone is like the kind of camaraderie where it's like, you're all in this team.
together and you're really like doing something that's like that team aspect.
Like I played team sports in high school.
Like I really enjoyed that.
Right.
And it's like, you kind of get that in your adult life if you like work at a startup.
And so I missed that a little bit.
Not a little bit, a lot bit.
And that was, that was a big thing of it that I was missing.
So it's like the learning aspect and then life of time, like being on a team.
I think the second aspect is like, I was very candidly fortunate in my situation where like my I said,
And then, right, fiance, now husband, like, he had a very stable career, like, he had a stable job.
And so he was, I was able to kind of go out on this limb.
And we could kind of like hunker down in that way.
But then he had a, he was kind of going through a career shift towards the end of 2022 as well.
And so I was like, you know what, like, I have a lot to learn.
This is like my time to like, have like a more stable gig so that you can kind of figure that out.
And so that was kind of like the role shift, the little bit that happened.
And so now we're so he did that.
And so now we're both being a freelancer, and he's like, being a lawyer, like, yeah, yeah, No, literally, yeah, yeah.
So now we're both thankfully in this position where like, he now has, you know, his own career, he's growing and I have mine.
Sounds pretty cool.
I want to meet him one day.
That's awesome.
But that's, that's, that's really cool.
And then why, why that company?
Why that?
Yeah, I think for me, I think it's always very important to add unique value.
And the unique value that I added there was like, I knew the company and its competitors.
And so, yes, I would be learning a lot on the marketing side, but on the space, like I could just contribute so much.
And so I wanted to put myself in that position where I was both felt like I was contributing a lot, but then also learning a lot.
And I think that that was just like a really great opportunity to do that.
think the other companies that I was evaluating, they were more like traditional marketing roles and less technical companies.
And I was like, it just, you know, I could go B2C, but like, I'm not a TikToker.
Like, I just don't have quite that like level of like creativity and like flash, you know what I mean?
And so it was more so just like, yeah, I think.
Um, uh, I think like B2B just makes sense for, I kind of understand the space, um, and kind of really can get behind it.
Yeah, that's awesome.
Um, well, like, tell me, tell me more, like, especially that first year before you, what looks like you, you were promoted.
Uh, tell me more a little bit, like the mandate, what the team was like, you know, give me a kind of a lay of the land and kind of what you focused on.
Um, totally.
Um, so that, um, that first year, um, so the team was two people.
So it's myself and my manager.
He was the head of growth.
Um, and we were honestly like, yes, he was my manager, but like by the end of the year, we were peers.
And so I think it was just like, he happened to manage me, but like, we just worked on a lot of projects together.
Um, he, um, uh, we were more focused on, we were very much focused on product growth is where the year started.
And so the mandate was like, uh,
So him and myself should own Seltzer.
We should be figuring out how to grow the product and like using the internal team for right information on how to do that.
I think that fell apart a little bit in the middle of the year when we were like having this, wait, we're not PLG.
We're actually a sale.
Like we need to be a sales led company.
And so there was a very big like switcheroo.
And that's where I was like, okay, there's clearly marketing to be done because we need to figure out like, we shouldn't be a growth team.
should be a marketing team and figuring out like, what are we saying?
And who are we saying it to?
So then he took on, what are we saying?
So he took on product marketing and I took on, who are we saying it to?
we're on the distribution side.
That's really cool.
I like that.

And then like in that second year, if you will, or in the last 12 months or there, like, well, like tell me more about your role, kind of how that, how things evolve after that, especially after that.
That junction point of going from, like, PLG only to more of, like, kind of a sales assist motion, if you will.
Yeah, so I would say where my role changed the most is we were thinking about, like, who's, like, getting eyes.
It's not just about getting eyes.
It's, like, whose eyes are most important.
And so we were, like, okay, well, we all have big companies.
So when we do any sort of outbound, we want to make sure that we're targeting big companies.
But we weren't really positioned well and didn't have the right team to do outbound.
And so that kind of was, a little bit down the water.
And so at the beginning of the year, so my counterpart, right, who was owning product, I guess, he left for a new opportunity.
And so I was a marketing team of one.
And so then it was, like, prioritizing, right, okay, what are the goals?
And then from the goals, we deduce the priorities.
so big.
Big.
thing was content.
it was like, okay, SEO, but then like technical content.
so I, I like couldn't do it all myself.

And so I hired a few, an agency, an SEO agency, and then also a, a few freelancers who like really do the space to do deeper technical content, I would say that like, there was that where we supported on the content side.
But then the big, the big shift was from getting product signups to, leads, and like sales accepted leads.
And so there was a lot of work on like, okay, how are we going like sales offs, right?
Like enrichment, and lead scoring, like all that type of stuff.
Like, who should we focus on?
Like, who should A's be going after?
And how can we build it, like spin up a warm outbound motion?
And so those were like the two biggest focus areas of kind of the, I would say the first half of 2024.
And.
And the latter half of 2024 was more around, so there was also a lot of team buildings, the team went from one to eight at the end of 2024.

And so then it was around, okay, like, how do we run cohesive campaigns and have a lot of, have more structure around like what we're saying, who we're saying it to, to kind of build a network and not just like be kind of just throwing random  out and seeing what sticks.
And so that was kind of the theme of the kind of the second half of 2024 and into 2025, which is like that cohesion around messaging, around campaigns and stuff like that.
That's cool.

And one thing I always like to ask my teams, and that was a good one, is like, when we're doing retro or doing planning, fiscal planning, quarterly planning, or priority planning, as like, if you look back in the last 12 months of being there, what was the most impactful thing you and the team did?
If you could all, you could have gone back in time and, but only do.
One initiative or one thing, know, regardless if that thing is big or small, you know, what was the most impactful thing you felt like you and the team did?
Yeah, I think the most impactful thing was actually the last thing that I did in February.
I wish we had done that way earlier, is like running a true integrated campaign.
This one was competitor driven.
So tying back to the first conversation or the first question around what was it, right?
What inhibited prefects growth?
And so it was like, we're putting out competitor content.
We are putting out migration guides.
We're like outbounding to people that use the competitor, like that we know use the competitor because the list is out there saying here's a migration guide, right?
And so it's like, it's then it starts to feel not just like activities, but it starts to feel way more like a way more like a cohesive plan.
And so that's.
That was starting in January.
We actually executed in February, and the plan was to continue to execute in March and then measure in April.
But based on how we were performing in February, we were on track to generate, I think it was like 1 million in pipeline just from that one campaign.
And it's like, if we had done that consistently, we wouldn't be in the position that we were in now.
Yeah, yeah, that's cool.
I mean, it's good learning, if you will, of an integrated campaign approach.
That's really interesting.
And then, like kind of in the last year or so, were you reporting to the CEO or who were you reporting to?
Yeah, that's an interesting question.
So I was reporting into a, so, okay, so in the last year, so I started reporting into the COO, and then we hired a VP of marketing who reported into the COO, and then I reported to
The VP of marketing.
And then things didn't work out with the VP of marketing six months later.
So then I reported again to the COO.
And then towards the end of 2024, I started reporting into the chief business officer who was promoted from finance.
And so he gained some responsibilities.
So he gained sales and growth.
Of those three, who do you feel like you spend the most time with?
Definitely the chief business officer.
Like, even when I wasn't reporting into him, like, he was a thought partner.
We worked very close to being together.
He, in the end, like, became actually a pretty close friend.
And so we just, like, really buy.
What was his first name?
Thomas.
Thomas.
And then how would Thomas describe you?
Yeah, Thomas would describe me as fast executor.
I think Thomas would describe me as very passionate.
I think he would describe.
He me as a strong leader, and I think he would describe me as a strong communicator.

So if you were, Thomas, yourself, and you were writing coaching notes for yourself for the next six months, things to focus on to continue to grow personally, like, or the things you would prioritize or tell yourself?
Yeah, I'm a little cheating, because I had this conversation with Thomas, so know some of his answers.
But it would definitely be, I think, in terms of, like, managing up, I think I was doing a lot of that.
But to be even more effective, to...
Keep in mind the altitude at which I'm talking.
So it's like don't always go into tactical.
That doesn't always matter.
Really focus on impact.
Like it's the way that I think, but sometimes I just get so excited about something that I just like go a little bit too long in the tactical.
And so it's really just like stay on impact.
You know what impact is.
Stay there.
That was kind of a big note.

I think the other thing is when observing something that like isn't going the way you think it should or like a problem that you see, get the right people on board instead of just like talking to some people about it.
Like be more critical about who is the right person to get on board to actually make a change.
And I think that as soon as he had that conversation with me, that was back in December.
I think the last three months, like there were a lot of situations when like I just thought I thought about it before like saying something like, oh, I don't like this.
Like that's not very productive.
But I thought about it and I was like, okay, this is a DevRel problem.
So I should speak to the head of DevRel and I should speak to DevRel's biggest stakeholder, right?
Which is like, right product, for example, right?
Just as an example.
And I think that allowed me to have much more productive conversations and much more impact in actually like making change.
Yeah, that makes sense.
Well, super, super helpful.
And hopefully that I always like these things because it's like introspective.
And so it's like, I always enjoy them.
But one of the things like that I'm really curious, because I think one thing that's obviously like really unique about you is that it's your background in data engineering.
And then like you started writing and, you know, sharing knowledge and being really passionate about that.
And then going and being really curious and wanting to learn.
Like, if you, you know,

You if you're writing your own job description, like, what would be, like, I know I kind of asked you that last time, but it's like, now that you have a little bit more context, too, like, what would be the perfect role for you, you know?
Yeah, I think the perfect role for me would be achieve a goal in the most repeatable, like, build a machine to achieve a goal.

Like, that sounds vague, but, like, in the context of marketing, it's, like, that's why I get excited about, like, distribution and tactics is because it's very measurable and it's, like, repeatability is the name of the game.
Like, you're trying to find something that you can then repeat and scale.
Like, if you can do that, you win.
And so, for me, like, I'm going to work really well in an environment where there is, like, a known value.
really a new, um, um...
And then I need to, like, figure out how to repeat and scale that.

And so I think whether that's, like, with the customer, whether that's finding new customers, whether that's, like, shouting about it from the rooftops, about, like, how it's been done with other people.
Like, I think for me, that's going to be the most important thing.
So there's, like, tons of different flavors of that.
But that's really the underlying theme.
Yeah, that's cool.
So, like, as we're talking, like, kind of what I'm thinking here is, like, right now we have several needs.
One of them being we have a lot of customers coming in that are, like, highly technical and serve technical audiences, right?
Like, we're closing a deal with SentinelOne.
You know, we have abnormal security.
We have, like, we GetPod.
We have, like, Galileo AI.
I'm sure I'm missing a bunch, but anyway, it's like maybe like 40% of our customers are actually like technical audiences, right?
And so because of that, we don't have the muscle of like building and figuring out how to deliver technical content that hits the bar in a way that's more programmatic and more repeatable, right?

So I think that in and of itself, it's like a challenge already that like you've experienced and but then like also you have the both the taste and the experience, you know, what good and great looks like, you know, which that part is also tough, right?

Like even if you're technical, you might not have the taste of like having done it yourself, you know, and written great content or, you know, done it for yourself and seeing like kind of having the empathy for what does it take to produce great content out of good knowledge.
knowledge solid knowledge, right?
There's a difference between like good knowledge.

Knowledge and information and good knowledge and information that is technical presented in a way that appeals to a technical audience in a way that doesn't come across as arrogant or, you know, but also approachable, but also not talking down to the audience and all those things, right?
Like, but that also shows that you're an expert in that subject, you know, and so that's one challenge and a massive opportunity.
Um, and then the other one is really like around, like kind of call it like go to market engineering, if you will.
Right.
And, and kind of like that, that function overall of like, uh, for ourselves as well.
Right.
Like, um, and, and then the other thing that I think it's kind of cool is like, you've had the entrepreneurial like bug, you will, and that tends to not go away.
So I don't know if like, there's no bad, like kind of answer here, but is that like something that you, you see yourself doing down the line?
It's not a trick question, promise it.

Yeah, no, maybe, but what I realized is I'm like, I don't know, just right now, like, there's just so many other things going on in my life, and I'm not ready to devote, like, to put literally everything on the line to start my own company.
And so, like, not right now is the answer.
I also, in hindsight, like, if I start my own company, like, I don't know if it would be in tech, to be honest.
Like, my husband and have talked about starting, like, an N.A.
beverage company.
Like, you know what I mean?
Like, and so, but it's definitely, for sure, not right now, and, like, very likely not within the next five years.
So, like, I can't speak for forever, but that's my answer for Anna.

Yeah, and, like, the reason I bring it up is, like, we just hired Jason as head of GoToMarket, and, but he's also going to be a managing director for some accounts, because, like, everyone's going be a managing director.
Like, you have to know what it takes to do a good job for clients, and know, like, you know.
Also product development.
And so I do think there's like some role here that's like part managing director for technical accounts, but by very quickly and in parallel, like having functional responsibilities as well, right?
Like around on like go-to-market engineering, like client operations, like meaning automating and streamlining, like pre-sale, post-sales processes, like, you know, automating things.
mean, just from a data engineering alone, right?
Like there's so much of like things that we need to do.
I'll give you just some examples.
Like right now, like we have access to everyone's like Google search console.
Like we have all this data, right?
Like we have access to people's analytics, right?
But we're not doing anything to correlate that with the changes we make in the content, right?
And we should be able to do that a lot more, right?
Like, and then, and so there's quite a bit there.
There's
So I think the idea here is not only doing all those like a big job, the managing director function, but also thinking more broadly and how to enable everything.
To your point, I want to build a machine to achieve the goal, and that's why you get excited about distribution is figuring out what works and then scaling that.
Like in a lot of ways, we need to do that, not only because of your expertise and background and technical content as well, but we need to do that for everyone, right?
And so it's like there's a lot of like how do we figure out what works and build a machine around it and scale that to everyone else, right?
And I think a lot of the background that folks are here, there's a lot of background in content editorial.
Matthew is our chief content officer.
He ran TechCrunch for 10 years, right?
Like, you know, there's a lot of that.
Like Dave was at TripAdvisor or head of SEO there.
Like, so, but like it's less outside of Jason.
Like, on the kind of, like, a bit more technical background that then got into marketing, but can kind of think that way as well, you know?
Yeah, that makes a lot of sense.
mean, the search console side, that definitely comes to mind.
Another one is, like, okay, hooking up into people's CMS systems, and, like, there's so much, like, automation that we've done.
I think that makes a lot of sense.
I think that would be really exciting to me to, like, have that sort of, like, joint role.

So, yeah, I do have to hop here at the top of the hour, but I would love to understand, like, what next steps look like, and would love to also chat with, like, someone else on the team, one of the other managing directors as well.
Yeah, yeah, that'll be great.

I think it'll be good for you to talk to Kyle, who's our first customer and has been transitioning from managing all the accounts to, like, onboarding all the managing directors, and he's going to be transitioning into doing more of the sales side.
And so we'll set up that for you, and then that should be pretty much the last step.
And we can move super, super quickly after that.
Okay.
Yeah, that sounds great.
Awesome.
Well, happy Friday.
And I'm sure we'll talk soon.
Cool.
Thanks so much, Marcel.
Have a good weekend.
You too.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss potential role for Sarah KB at GrowthX, exploring her background and fit with company needs.](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Sarah has a unique background combining data engineering, technical content creation, and marketing leadership](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [GrowthX needs someone to build repeatable processes for technical content and go-to-market operations](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Potential hybrid role as managing director for technical accounts + functional responsibilities in go-to-market engineering](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Topics

### Sarah's Professional Background

  - [Data engineering roles at PerPay and other companies](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=1278.0)
  - [Transitioned to marketing/growth at Prefect, scaling team from 1 to 8 people](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=2365.0)
  - [Experience with content creation, SEO, lead generation, and integrated campaigns](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=2468.0)
  - [Strong technical background combined with marketing expertise](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=3014.0)

### GrowthX's Business Model and Strategy

  - [Services-first approach allows extracting more value and learning faster](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=395.0)
  - [Building internal tools/platforms that could become products later](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=583.0)
  - [Projecting 76-79% gross margin this year, 91% next year](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=540.0)
  - [Inspired by companies like Tiny, Amazon AWS, and DBT Labs](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=661.0)

### Potential Role at GrowthX

  - [Hybrid role: Managing director for technical accounts + functional responsibilities](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=3301.0)
  - [Focus on building repeatable processes for technical content creation](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=3129.0)
  - [Go-to-market engineering: automating/streamlining pre/post-sales, data analysis](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=3324.0)
  - [Leverage Sarah's technical background and marketing experience](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=3175.0)

### Sarah's Career Motivations

  - [Wants to "build a machine to achieve a goal" - find repeatable, scalable processes](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=3028.0)
  - [Values learning opportunities and being part of a team](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=2178.0)
  - [Not currently looking to start own company, but has entrepreneurial interests](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=3241.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Sarah to speak with Kyle (first GrowthX customer, transitioning to sales)](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=3459.0)
  - [Move quickly after that conversation to potentially make an offer](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=3480.0)
  - [Explore specifics of hybrid managing director + functional role](https://fathom.video/share/zPxEkqwqxjF2sxwHf84az5-YhJMqzzYR?tab=summary&timestamp=3429.0)

action_items.description: Arrange Sarah KB mtgs w/ Kyle (1st customer, onboarding MDs) + another MD. Discuss role/responsibilities, sales transition, tech content/accounts
